seo.title: ARCP — Agent Runtime Control Protocol
seo.description: A transport-agnostic wire protocol for submitting, observing, and controlling long-running AI agent jobs.

hero.headline: The durable envelope around long-running agent work.
hero.lede: ARCP is a transport-agnostic wire protocol for **submitting, observing, and controlling long-running AI agent jobs** — and nothing more.

sections.body:
ARCP defines the execution envelope around AI agent work: **sessions, jobs,
resumable event streams, capability-bounded leases, and delegation**. It
specifies how a job is submitted, how its events flow back, who is allowed to
do what, and how the whole thing survives a dropped connection.

It is deliberately narrow. ARCP wraps the agent function — it does not implement
the agent, expose its tools, or export its telemetry. Tool exposure is the concern
of [MCP](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/). Telemetry export is the concern of
[OpenTelemetry](https://opentelemetry.io/). ARCP composes with them rather than
absorbing them.

A job submitted over ARCP keeps running when the client disconnects, can be
resumed or watched live from another machine, and cannot exceed the `lease` it
was granted — a bound on what it may read, write, call, spend, and for how long.

Long-running agent jobs break the assumptions of a request/response API. They
outlive the connection that started them, act with real spending and filesystem
authority, and frequently need to be watched by more than one observer at once.
ARCP exists to make that envelope **explicit and enforceable at the wire level**,
so any client and any runtime can interoperate.

The protocol organizes every message under four concerns. Everything it
specifies is in service of one of them.

items.title: Identity
items.description: Who is acting. Authenticated sessions and a per-job submitting principal.

items.title: Durability
items.description: Work survives disconnects. Sequenced events, resume after a drop, subscribe from elsewhere.

items.title: Authority
items.description: What an agent may do, bounded by capability, budget, and an expiring lease.

items.title: Observability
items.description: Structured progress, logs, metrics, and streamed results — plus W3C trace context.

sections.body:
An interaction is a session that carries one or more jobs. The wire format is a
single JSON envelope; the transport is WebSocket for the network and stdio for
in-process children.

items.description: Open a transport and exchange `session.hello` → `session.welcome`. The client declares identity and supported features; the runtime returns a session, a resume token, and an agent inventory with versions.
Send `job.submit` carrying a `lease_request` — what the agent may read, write, call, spend, and until when. The runtime replies `job.accepted` with the effective lease.
Consume the `job.event` stream: logs, thoughts, tool calls, structured `progress`, cost `metric`s, and chunked `result_chunk`s for large outputs.
Reconnect with `session.resume` after a network drop, or `job.subscribe` from another session to watch a job live — without the authority to cancel it.
The job terminates with `job.result` or `job.error`. Leases that expire or budgets that run out fail closed.

code.title: job.submit
code.caption: A job bounded in space, money, model, and time. The runtime enforces it on every operation.

sections.body:
The clearest way to understand a protocol is to know where it stops. ARCP draws
a hard line and stays behind it.

items.label: The wire format for client–runtime communication
The lifecycle of sessions, jobs, and event streams
The authority model — leases with time and budget bounds
Scheduling, priority, or pause/resume of running jobs
How agent state persists across restarts
